@graph.name: Mercedes G500 / G550 Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes G500 / G550 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned G500/G550 engine typically costs £3,000–£7,500 supply only depending on generation. Fitting adds £2,500–£4,000 depending on location and workshop rates. A replacement motor of type 157.982 costs on average £7,908 in good condition. Main dealer new engines start from £12,000–£22,000+ supply only. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes G500 / G550 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends entirely on which engine generation your G-Class has. The M113 (1998–2008) is widely regarded as 'rock solid' and 'bulletproof' — the M113 motor found on the G500 from 2002 to 2013 is known to be rock solid from a reliability standpoint. The M273 (2009–2015) has balance shaft and camshaft adjuster problems. The M176 (2016–2024) has documented timing chain stretch and camshaft adjuster failures. Check the Era Map above for your specific engine.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes G500 / G550?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain tensioner wear on M113 engines (check at 100,000 miles), timing chain stretch and camshaft adjuster failure on M176 biturbo V8s, balance shaft and camshaft adjuster issues on M273 engines, and oil leaks from valve covers and oil filter housing across all generations.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes G500 / G550 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £15,000–£60,000 G-Class, a £5,000–£7,500 recon is 10–50% of the car's value. For a clean M113-engined example, replacement is almost always worth it given the engine's legendary durability and the G-Class's strong retained values. For newer M176 examples with documented service history, replacement is viable.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes G500 / G550 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: W463 (1998–2008) has M113 (5.0L V8), W463 (2009–2015) has M273 (5.5L V8), W463 (2016–2024) has M176 (4.0L biturbo V8), W463 (2025–present) has M256 (3.0L I6 MHEV). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent timing chain issues on the M113?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M113 is 'bulletproof' but the timing chain tensioner is the main weak point. It is generally accepted that timing chains should be checked and replaced as necessary at every 100,000 miles. Regular oil changes with the correct MB-spec oil and early attention to warning signs like rattling noises can help prevent costly engine damage. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately.

eraMap.title: Mercedes G500 / G550 — Which Engine Does Your Car Actually Have?

rows.eraNote: 5.0L V8 — 296 hp, 339 lb-ft. "Rock solid" and "bulletproof"; timing chain tensioner is the main weak point
5.5L V8 — 382 hp, 391 lb-ft. Balance shaft and camshaft adjuster issues documented
4.0L biturbo V8 — 416–422 hp, 450 lb-ft. Timing chain stretch and camshaft adjuster failures are key concerns
3.0L inline-six turbo with 48V mild hybrid — 443 hp, 560 Nm. Early reliability data still emerging

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes G500 / G550 Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a G-Class V8 engine typically takes 16–24 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The G-Class's heavy-duty chassis and body-on-frame construction add labour time.

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Failure (M113 — The Bulletproof Engine's Weakness)
problems.symptoms: Rattling or ticking noise from the engine on cold start, metallic noise that subsides as oil pressure builds, engine performance issues, check engine light
problems.typicalMileage: 100,000–200,000 miles — the M113 is known to last 200,000 miles, but tensioners can fail

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The M113 engine is "rock solid" and "bulletproof" — "the engine and transmission in the M113/722.6 G500s seem to mechanically go forever, so high mileage is probably of no concern here". However, the timing chain tensioner is the main weak point. It is generally accepted that timing chains should be checked and replaced as necessary at every 100,000 miles. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, have the tensioner inspected immediately. A £500–£1,500 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than a £4,500–£9,500 engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain tensioner guide → /failures/m113-timing-chain-tensioner/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

problems.title: Timing Chain & Camshaft Adjuster Issues (M176 Biturbo V8)
problems.symptoms: Rattling or knocking noise on cold start, check engine light, rough running, loss of power, camshaft correlation fault codes
problems.typicalMileage: 40,000–80,000 miles — can occur at lower mileages on high-performance variants

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The M176 biturbo V8 has documented timing chain stretch and camshaft adjuster (phaser) failures. Common issues include camshaft adjuster failure, timing chain stretch, timing disc displacement, turbocharger problems, and oil leaks from valve covers. At the first sign of cold-start rattling, have the camshaft adjusters and timing chain inspected immediately. A £1,500–£3,500 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than a £6,500–£12,000 engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide → /failures/m176-timing-chain/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

problems.title: Balance Shaft & Camshaft Adjuster Issues (M273 5.5L V8)
problems.symptoms: Check engine light, rough running, timing chain noise, loss of power, engine warning light

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling or see the check engine light, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The M273 5.5L V8 has well-documented balance shaft and camshaft adjuster issues. The 2009–2015 G550 uses this engine. If you experience rough running or timing chain noise, have the balance shaft and camshaft adjusters inspected immediately. A £2,000–£4,500 repair is far cheaper than a £5,500–£10,000 engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full balance shaft failure guide → /failures/m273-balance-shaft/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

problems.title: Oil Leaks (Valve Cover Gaskets & Oil Filter Housing)
problems.symptoms: Burning oil smell, oil spots under the car, visible oil residue around the valve covers or oil filter housing, low oil level warning
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,200 (valve cover gasket replacement) / £300–£800 (oil filter housing gasket)
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection if you notice oil spots or burning smell

problems.recommendation: Oil leaks are common across all G-Class V8 engines. The M176 is known for oil leaks from under the valve covers. If you notice a burning smell or oil spots, have the gaskets inspected promptly. Catching leaks early prevents oil contamination of other components and more serious engine damage.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil leak guide → /failures/g-class-oil-leaks/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: G500 / G550 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

buyingChecks.title: Buying This G500 / G550? Check These First
buyIf: * M113-engined G500 (1998–2008) — these are "rock solid" and "bulletproof"
* Documented timing chain and tensioner work on any engine
* Full service history with regular oil changes using MB-approved oil
* M176 with documented camshaft adjuster and timing chain work
* No cold-start rattling — this is a critical warning sign
avoidIf: * M273 (2009–2015) with no balance shaft or camshaft adjuster history
* M176 (2016–2024) with cold-start rattle and no timing chain history
* Poor service history — these are complex, high-performance engines that need proper maintenance
* Check engine light with timing-related codes

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The G500/G550 badge spans four very different engine generations. The M113 (1998–2008) is widely regarded as "rock solid" and "bulletproof" — the M113 motor found on the G500 from 2002 to 2013 is known to be rock solid from a reliability standpoint. The M273 (2009–2015) introduced balance shaft and camshaft adjuster problems. The M176 (2016–2024) is a modern biturbo V8 with documented timing chain stretch and camshaft adjuster failures. The M256 (2025–present) adds 48V mild-hybrid complexity. Buy a pre-2009 M113 if you want the most durable option. Buy a well-maintained later engine with documented service history. Buy on documented history, not on generation alone.

cta.label: → Compare G500 / G550 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes G500 or G550 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or ticking from the engine (timing chain tensioner / camshaft adjuster warning — this is critical)
* Service History — Including which engine era (M113, M273, M176 or M256) and confirmed oil-interval discipline
* Timing Chain History — Has the timing chain or tensioner been replaced? (M113: check at 100,000 miles; M176: camshaft adjuster history critical)
* Oil Leaks — Look for oil residue around the valve covers and oil filter housing
* Balance Shaft — M273 engines: any check engine light or timing noise? (Balance shaft issues)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Look for timing-related codes
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
* Warranty Status — Extended warranty is highly recommended for M176 and M256 engines
